channel.title: The Ark
channel.description: The Ark is the weekly newspaper of Tiburon, Belvedere and Strawberry

item.title: Supervisors greenlight Strawberry seminary redevelopment after years of conflict
item.description: The project calls for 336 homes on the hilltop campus, including 70 below-market-rate units, plus a 150-unit senior care facility and a graduate school.
item.content:encoded:
Updated:23 minutes ago

The Marin County Board of Supervisors has approved the long-disputed redevelopment of the former Golden Gate Baptist Theological Seminary campus in Strawberry, clearing the way for hundreds of homes, a senior-care center and a graduate school.

At its June 9 meeting, the board voted unanimously to approve the project after several hours of staff presentation. Public comment drew 26 speakers, most of whom criticized the proposal.

The board adopted the staff recommendation in full, certifying the project's environmental review and approving its master plan, design and permits. It denied a related amendment to the Strawberry Community Plan, which staff said would not reduce the housing because the developer can rely on state density-bonus law instead.

The decision comes after more than a decade of conflict that pitted Strawberry neighbors against developer North Coast Land Holdings, with the county in the middle. The approval clears North Coast to move forward with its building-permit application.

Before the planning commission's March 2 hearing, North Coast reached an agreement with the Seminary Neighborhood Association, which had long opposed the development. On March 30 the commission recommended approval, but some residents said they felt left out.

In her presentation to the board, Principal Planner Michelle Levenson called the proposal "the final chapter" in a yearslong debate. She said the process had drawn substantial input from the public and stakeholders, including more than 50 meetings as part of the Seminary Tomorrow process.

Staff said the project is shaped by California housing laws that sharply limit the county's ability to deny or shrink the proposal. The environmental impact report found three significant and unavoidable effects: greenhouse gas emissions, temporary construction noise, and transportation effects tied to vehicle miles traveled. Most other impacts could be mitigated.

The agreement between the developer and the Seminary Neighborhood Association added conditions limiting academic use, capping commuting students at 325 unless additional students live on site, and restricting the daycare and fitness center primarily to residents and on-site users. County staff and an attorney for North Coast said the agreement is enforceable as a condition of approval, not a private deal.

The project calls for 336 homes on the hilltop campus, which overlooks San F...
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister

item.title: Tiburon boosts parks funding in draft $28.38 million budget
item.description: Parents for Parks and Rec pushed the council for more funding, saying the town's five-year plan includes little money for meaningful new park improvements.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon officials directed staff to put an additional $1.5 million toward parks in next year’s budget, responding to residents who pressed the town for a firmer commitment to its recreational amenities.

The Town Council gave the direction June 3 as it reviewed a draft $28.38 million budget for fiscal year 2026-2027. The council took no final action, and its directives will trim that figure to about $27.88 million in the revised budget it is scheduled to adopt June 17. Councilmember Holli Thier was absent.

The $1.5 million combines $1 million transferred from the town’s general-fund reserves with $500,000 freed by dropping a planned repair of the Elephant Rock fishing pier. Because the reserve money moves between town accounts rather than adding to spending, the budget total falls rather than rises. That $1.5 million is on top of $1 million already set to move into the parks fund in fiscal 2027, which begins July 1.

Parks and their costs ran through much of the discussion, and councilmembers also signaled openness to creating a finance committee to guide the town’s long-term revenue planning.

The draft’s $28.38 million total includes $19.13 million in day-to-day operating expenditures, nearly $8 million for infrastructure upgrades under the town’s capital-improvement program and $1.25 million in debt service. Dropping the $500,000 pier project will lower the revised budget returning June 17 to about $27.88 million and its associated capital-improvement program to roughly $7.5 million, Town Manager Greg Chanis said after the meeting.

Operating expenditures run just under the $19.15 million in revenue, a $21,642 surplus. Expenditures and revenue are each projected to rise 4.79% from the current fiscal 2026 budget.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Neighbors lose bid to roll back pickleball hours at Tiburon Peninsula Club
item.description: Public hearings over pickleball at the private club have stretched nearly three years, rooted in a 2019 dispute over noise and unpermitted play.
item.content:encoded:
Expanded pickleball hours at the Tiburon Peninsula Club are here to stay after the Town Council rejected an appeal from neighbors who complained about the sport’s noise and its impact on their homes and lives.

Councilmembers spent nearly two hours hearing from club members and opposing neighbors before voting 2-1 at their June 3 meeting to uphold the Planning Commission’s decision to expand playing hours to seven days a week and allow longer hours in spring and summer. The vote rejected an appeal from Mar West Street resident Eric Pifer.

Vice Mayor Isaac Nikfar and Councilmember Jack Ryan voted in favor, while Mayor Jon Welner cast the dissenting vote, siding with neighbors in saying “there is a qualitative difference with the kind of noise that you get in pickleball” compared with other nearby sounds.

As a result of the council’s vote, the private club will have expanded pickleball hours of 9 a.m.-6:30 p.m. Mondays and Wednesdays through Saturdays from March 1 to Oct. 31, and 9 a.m.-5 p.m. those days from November through February. Mondays had previously been off limits.

Play will also be allowed 10 a.m.-4:30 p.m. Sundays year-round, a 2½-hour increase, and 9 a.m.-4 p.m. Tuesdays year-round, a one-hour reduction. Commissioners trimmed the Tuesday hours at their March 25 hearing to give neighbors some respite from the noise.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Taco trouble: A two-week craze sparked debate over health rules, entrepreneurship and what residents want
item.description: A Mexican food stand that drew long lines and rave reviews on Tiburon Boulevard was shut down after two weeks over health-code violations including no permit and no refrigeration.
item.content:encoded:
Taqueria El Chachoset up shop at Tiburon Boulevard’s inbound intersection with Greenwood Cove Drive, next to Westminster Presbyterian Church, in mid-May.It quickly built a social-media following online, thanks to its street-food classics: tacos de asada, burritos al pastor and quesadillas con chorizo.

However, as customers lined up, complaints also came in. The Marin County Sheriff’s Office received seven calls about the stand between May 15 and May 31, with callers questioning its legality and safety practices.

County health officials, with support from the Sheriff’s Office, on May 29 confiscated what one official described as “hundreds of pounds of food” amid several health-code violations at the Tiburon Boulevard site: no permit, no handwashing stations, no refrigeration and no clear source for the food or details on how it was prepared.

Locals on Nextdoor were up in arms. A post about the stand’s May 29 shutdown drew 216 reactions and 141 comments, with many supporting the vendor and some raising concerns about health standards.

“I knew this would happen. Was just waiting for a Karen or Ken to freak out,” Reedlands resident Maryam Fooman said in a comment. “Let them be. They are just trying to make a living. You don’t feel like it’s safe to eat there, then don’t. Shame on you.”

On May 30, the stand reappeared on the other side of Tiburon Boulevard near the 76 gas station.

The stand was shut down for good May 31, though a handmade cardboard sign remained posted at its original location near the church: “The people want tacos.”

“We’re going to look (for) the way to move forward and be there in Tiburon as well,”owner MiguelLopez, a 20-year-old L.A. resident, said in a June 4 interview conducted in both English and Spanish.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Taqueria El Chacho in its two-week presence at the corner of Tiburon Boulevard and Greenwood Cove Drive, seen here May 27, drew rave reviews and long lines from locals looking to try their food. The street-food stand was shut down twice following complaints over unpermitted food selling, which led to an outcry on local forums like Nextdoor. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark)

After Taqueria El Chacho was shut down May 31 at the Tiburon Boulevard-Greenwood Cove Drive intersection near the 76 gas station, devoted fans put up a message of support for the taqueria, saying ‘the people want tacos,’ at its original location near Westminster Presbyterian Church, seen here June 1. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark)

item.title: Peninsula kicks off summer with street fest — then car show, boat show, Juneteenth and free concert
item.description: Friday Nights on Main is June 12, the annual Tiburon Classic Car Show is June 20, then June 21 is the wooden boat show, Juneteenth and Belvedere Concert in the Park.
item.content:encoded:
The peninsula’s summer season kicks off over the next two weekends, starting with Friday Night on Main on June 12, when lower Main Street will close to traffic for an evening of live music, children’s activities and outdoor dining.

The festivities continue Father’s Day weekend with the Tiburon Classic and Exotic Car Show, a wooden boat show, a Juneteenth celebration on Main Street and a concert in Belvedere Community Park.

The Tiburon Peninsula Chamber of Commerce will spark the summer with the first of two Friday Nights on Main, from 5:30 to 8:30 p.m. June 12. The event falls on the last day of school for the Reed Union School District.Lower Main Street will be closed to traffic for live music, children’s activities and alfresco food and drinks.

The following weekend packs in four events: a car show, a wooden boat show, a Juneteenth celebration and a Neil Diamond tribute concert.

On June 20, Tiburon will rev up for the 26th annual Tiburon Classic and Exotic Car Show, from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. at Shoreline Park.The big draw this year will be Cadillac’s first Formula 1 race car.

June 21 is Father’s Day, and three events fill the day.

The Corinthian Yacht Club will host its annual Master Mariners Wooden Boat Show from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. at 43 Main St.Two historic boats from Spaulding Marine Center will be among those on display.

Tiburon’s Diversity Inclusion Task Force will celebrate Juneteenth from 3 to 7 p.m. on lower Main Street. The celebration will feature live jazz, hip-hop, poetry readings and speakers. Hip-Hop For Change, an Oakland-based nonprofit that works with marginalized youth to reclaim the roots of hip-hop culture, will present poetry and an interactive performance.

The peninsula will wrap up the weekend with a June 21 concert by Super Diamond, a Neil Diamond tribute band, at 4 p.m. at Belvedere Community Park. The opening act will be a local rock band from Del Mar Middle School.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister

item.title: Tiburon to celebrate Fourth of July with downtown block party, Pooch Parade
item.description: Tiburon will mark the nation's 250th birthday with a July 4 block party on lower Main Street from 4 to 8 p.m., capped by a waterfront view of San Francisco's fireworks.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon will mark 250 years of American independence on the Fourth of July with a Main Street block party that includes a costumed dog parade, live music and kids’ activities. Attendees can then stay downtown to catch a waterfront viewing of San Francisco’s fireworks show later that evening.

The festivities, organized by the town and the Tiburon Peninsula Chamber of Commerce, are planned for 4-8 p.m. July 4 on lower Main Street, which will be closed to car traffic 1-9 p.m. The Town Council approved the closure by a 4-0 vote at its June 3 meeting, with Councilmember Holli Thier absent.

“I think it sounds really fun and great to do,” Mayor Jon Welner said of the event.

The block party will kick off at 4 p.m. with the Pooch Parade, with owners walking their patriotic-costumed dogs from the corner of Tiburon Boulevard and Beach Road, along Ark Row and down lower Main Street before finishing at Fountain Plaza, where owners can take photos with their dogs. Parade participants will receive a gift from the chamber.

Dogs must beregistered onlineto participate in the parade.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Del Mar students tackle inaugural Math Festival
item.description: Del Mar Middle School held its inaugural Math Festival June 1, with students taking on logic challenges, visual puzzles and complex problem-solving.
item.content:encoded: From top, left to right, Del Mar Middle School students Olivia Glassell and Anders Eggan; Charley Samaras and Serena Scully; and Nathan Ahlsten, Surya Allidina, Nicholas Pearce and Theo Neland take part in the school’s inaugural Math Festival June 1.Del Mar Middle School students Nathan Ahlsten, Surya Allidina, Nicholas Pearce and Theo Neland take part in the school’s inaugural Math Festival June 1. Students took on logic challenges, visual puzzles, pattern exploration and complex problem-solving. Amy Smith, the Reed Union School District’s math teacher on special assignment, said the festival aimed to let students experience math in a different way through hands-on activities and puzzles that emphasize curiosity, creativity, problem-solving and collaboration. ‘We wanted students to see that math extends beyond the classroom and can be engaging, challenging and fun,’ she said. Smith said she hopes students walked away with ‘greater confidence in their mathematical abilities’ and ‘discovered that being successful in math is not about finding answers quickly. It’s about thinking critically, taking risks, learning from mistakes and enjoying the process of solving challenging problems.’ (Clara Lu / For The Ark)
item.dc:creator: The Ark

item.title: Wellness app aims to help teen girls build confidence, connection
item.description: Tiburon resident Jessica Etchevers, a former school psychologist, and Mill Valley resident Ila Panik Marchetta launched the app after teaming up at a fundraiser.
item.content:encoded:
For many teen girls, social media is a constant part of their daily routine, whether scrolling Instagram, sharing videos on TikTok or messaging friends on Snapchat.

That engagement can foster connection and self-discovery, but research suggests it can also take a toll on teen girls’ mental and emotional health. Some 45% of U.S. teens ages 13-17 said they spend too much time on social media, according to a Pew Research Center survey published last year, with teen girls more likely than boys to report harmful effects. Some 25% of girls said social media was more likely to hurt their mental health, compared with 14% of boys; 20% of girls said it negatively affects their confidence, compared with 10% of boys; and 50% said it was detrimental to their sleep, versus 40% of boys.

Tiburon resident Jessica Etchevers and Mill Valley resident Ila Panik Marchetta are aiming to counter that negativity and change the way teen girls use social media withU4U, a new social wellness appthat offers short daily check-ins, activities and real-time peer support to help girls ages 11-18 build confidence and a sense of belonging.

The goal of the free app, which is available via U4U’s website and launched in Apple’s App Store last month, is to offer some of the appeal of traditional social media in a healthier, more guided way, Panik Marchetta says.

“Let’s look at why girls are so addicted to that, and how can we do something like that in a way that’s going to make their lives better, that’s going to help them become mentally healthy, become leaders and fulfill their potential,” she says.
item.dc:creator: Emily Lavin
item.media:description: Mill Valley resident Ila Panik Marchetta (left) and Tiburon resident Jessica Etchevers are the co-founders of U4U, a new social wellness app that offers short daily check-ins, activities and real-time peer support to help girls ages 11-18 build confidence and a sense of belonging. The free app is available via U4U’s website and launched in Apple’s App Store in May. (Clara Lu / For The Ark)

item.title: Everyday Encounters: A Tiburon resident is happy to be back after five decades
item.description: Portner grew up partly in Venezuela, speaking Spanish, before her family moved to Tiburon when she was 13. She says Venezuelan culture remains a core part of her identity.
item.content:encoded:
Kim Portner lived away from Tiburon for nearly 50 years. Six months ago, she moved back.

“I’ve always thought about it,” she says. “This was just in my heart.”

Portner, 64, takes in the view of San Francisco Bay from a bench at Shoreline Park June 2. She says it takes her back to her early teenage years. Portner moved to Tiburon with her family when she was 13, attending Del Mar Middle School and Redwood High School for two years before her family relocated to Los Angeles. But her years in Tiburon stuck with her, she says.

“I have super beautiful memories of my time here,” she says. “And it was so idyllic. I loved going to the library. I loved riding my bike. I loved going to Paradise Beach and taking the ferry to Angel Island.”

In the early 1970s, things were different, she says, pointing toward the Tiburon ferry landing.

“I had the best memories of watching the ferries go in and out,” she says. “Back then you could literally dangle your feet over the dock and just watch them come in and out.”
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: Kim Portner stands along the Tiburon waterfront June 2, six months after returning to town following nearly 50 years away. Portner moved to Tiburon with her family at 13 and lived in El Paso, New York, Seattle and the Florida Keys before coming back. (Tyler Callister / For The Ark)

item.title: Pick of the Week: Opera arias with Caroluna at Old St. Hilary’s Landmark
item.description: Lyric soprano Caroluna will return to Old St. Hilary’s Landmark for a concert at 3 p.m. June 14.
item.content:encoded: Lyric soprano Caroluna will return to Old St. Hilary’s Landmark for a concert at 3 p.m. June 14. Accompanied by classical pianist Jennifer M. Lee, she will perform opera arias and songs by Puccini, Bellini, Mozart, Dvořák, Saint-Saëns, Bizet, Chopin and Pauline Viardot, a 19th-century composer and soprano. Tickets are $20-$25 in advance at the Landmarks Society office or online, $25-$30 at the door if not sold out. There is no parking at Old St. Hilary’s. Free parking is available at The Boardwalk shopping center, with a shuttle departing from in front of Rustic Bakery beginning half an hour before the show. 415-435-1853 orlandmarkssociety.com.
item.dc:creator: The Ark
item.media:description: Pianist Jennifer M. Lee (left) will accompany singer Caroluna June 14 at Old St. Hilary’s Landmark. (Handout photo)

item.title: Measure E: Mill Valley schools funding measure cruising to victory
item.description: Mill Valley School District's Measure E parcel tax is passing with 70.37% in preliminary returns, just above the two-thirds threshold it needs.
item.content:encoded:
Mill Valley School District voters are backing Measure E, a parcel tax that would fund roughly a quarter of the district’s budget for the next eight years. The initiative would spare Strawberry Point Elementary and the district’s other schools from the staffing cuts officials warned would follow a defeat.

Measure E was ahead with 73.6% of the vote as of June 8, above the two-thirds majority it needs to pass. Of the 8,630 ballots cast across the district’s 12 precincts, 6,352 favored the tax and 2,278 opposed it. In Strawberry, Measure E led with 70.6% support, with 1,644 votes in favor and 686 opposed across the community’s two precincts. A single Tiburon precinct, covering the Cypress Hollow neighborhood, also falls within the district, where the measure drew 77.8% support, 42 votes to 12. Most of Tiburon lies in the Reed Union School District and did not vote on Measure E.

Additional returns were expected to be posted June 10, after The Ark’s press deadline.

Mill Valley School District board President Natalie Katz said the early results felt like “a meaningful vote of confidence from our community” at a critical time for the district, citing increasing enrollment across the district’s six elementary and middle school campuses. “Mill Valley is a community that has always understood that great public schools are one of the key pillars of what makes this an extraordinary place to live,” she said. “Strong schools attract families, support local businesses and enrich the broader community in ways that benefit everyone.” She added that voters recognized the measure was “not just about school funding” but “about the kind of community we want to be and the legacy we want to leave for the next generation.”

Measure E would replace the district’s existing parcel tax with a single combined levy of $1,754 per parcel, beginning July 1 and running through June 30, 2034. It merges two pieces: a renewal of the district’s primary parcel tax, now $1,520 per parcel, and a restored $234 levy that voters first approved in 2012 for an eight-year term that expired in 2021. The restored levy would return at its original rate, with no adjustment for the years it lapsed.

The county’s impartial analysis estimated the tax would generate about $14.9 million a year, about one-quarter of the district’s $58 million budget. The $1,754 rate would hold through the first year, with a 5% annual increase starting July 2027. Seven such increases would push...
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister

item.title: Measure B: SMART tax extension wins broad support
item.description: Voters in Marin and Sonoma counties are approving SMART's Measure B by a wide margin, with 70.33% in favor in preliminary returns from Tuesday's primary.
item.content:encoded:
A 30-year extension of the Sonoma-Marin Area Rail Transit District’s quarter-cent sales tax is passing comfortably in updated returns from the June 2 primary, with about 73% of voters across the two-county district in favor.

Measure B had 102,711 votes in support and 38,931 opposed, or 27.5%, with all precincts reporting in both counties, as of updates posted June 8 in Marin and June 5 in Sonoma. The citizens’ initiative needs a simple majority to pass.

Support was similar in both counties: 73.6% in Marin and 71.8% in Sonoma. On the Tiburon Peninsula, the measure drew 73.1% support, with 3,748 votes in favor and 1,377 opposed. By community, Strawberry voted 75.3% in favor, Tiburon 71.9% and Belvedere 68.8%.

The measure would not raise taxes. It would continue the same quarter-cent rate Marin and Sonoma voters approved in 2008, set to expire in 2029, and extend it through March 31, 2059. SMART estimates the tax would generate about $51 million a year.

Proceeds would fund rail operations and maintenance, capital improvements and planned extensions toward Healdsburg and Cloverdale. SMART operates passenger trains from Larkspur to Windsor, with a bicycle-pedestrian pathway alongside the tracks, and reported more than 1.1 million passengers and 146,000 bicycle trips in 2025.

Results remain preliminary. The next update is scheduled for June 10, after The Ark’s print deadline.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Hessel

item.title: Election roundup: Tiburon’s Thier concedes, Lucan leads in Assembly field; Steyer has edge among local voters
item.description: Tiburon Councilmember Holli Thier has conceded the 12th Assembly District primary, running fourth with 14% of the vote.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon Councilmember Holli Thier has conceded the race to succeed Assemblymember Damon Connolly in the 12th Assembly District, running fourth in the six-candidate field in preliminary returns from the June 2 primary.

Thier, a Democrat, had 14.2% of the districtwide vote as of June 8.

“I’m proud of the strong campaign we built and to be running second among all Democratic candidates in election-night returns for Marin County. I want to thank my supporters for your time, your talent, your contributions, but most importantly your belief in me to implement our shared vision for a better California. I’ll always be grateful for your support and that and our work has just begun.”

Thier is running second among Democrats in Marin but third among Democrats across the full district, which also includes parts of Sonoma County.

With five candidates registered as Democrats, the split field on the left appears to have opened a pathway for Eryn Cervantes, the lone Republican on the ballot, to reach the Nov. 3 general election. The top two vote-getters in the primary, regardless of party, qualify for November’s race.

Marin Supervisor Eric Lucan, D-Novato, leads the field with 27.7% of the vote. Cervantes, a corrections counselor at San Quentin Rehabilitation Center, is second at 22.2%. Rohnert Park Councilmember Jackie Elward, a Democrat, is third at 20.5%, with Thier fourth at 14.2%. Corte Madera Councilmember Eli Beckman, a Democrat, has 8.6% and Sebastopol nonprofit executive Steve Schwartz, a Democrat, 6.8%.

The district extends into Sonoma County, including Rohnert Park and parts of Petaluma. Connolly is termed out of office and running for state Senate in District 2.

The next update is expected June 10, after The Ark’s print deadline.

Lucan’s lead rested on Marin, where he took 34.3% of the county’s vote. In Sonoma County, Elward led with 28.2%, Cervantes was second at 27% and Lucan third at 17.9%. Marin leaned more Democratic, giving the five Democrats a combined 81.1% to Cervantes’ 18.9%; in Sonoma, Democrats drew 73% to Cervantes’ 27%. Across the district, the five Democrats took 77.8% of the vote, reflecting the party’s strength in the district.

On the Tiburon Peninsula, the top of the race was tight. Lucan led with 28.7% of the 4,712 votes cast, followed by Thier at 23.6% and Cervantes at 22.8%.

Thier ran strongest close to home. In Tiburon, she led with 596 votes to Lucan’s and Cervantes’...
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Tiburon plans $8M year of park, infrastructure improvements
item.description: Tiburon plans to spend $8M in fiscal 2027 on streets, parks and storm drains, part of a $21.2M five-year infrastructure program.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon plans to spend $8 million in fiscal 2026-2027 on projects ranging from street paving and storm-drain repairs to adaptive traffic signals and park design, part of a $21.2 million five-year program focused largely on maintaining the town’s existing infrastructure.

About $1.49 million is set aside for parks- and trail-related improvements and design efforts, including finishing designs for two of the major sites the town is looking to improve: Cypress Hollow Park and the former sanitation ponds site near McKegney Green.

The plan does not appropriate funds but serves as a budgeting and planning tool. The Town Council is scheduled to review the budget, including the capital-improvement plan, at 6:30 p.m. June 3, with adoption expected June 17 ahead of the new fiscal year beginning July 1.

Public Works Operations Manager Patrick Kerslake said before the meeting that projected costs for the coming year’s program are locked in, although fund balances may be revised ahead of final adoption.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Artist welcomes all reactions to ‘Word on the Street’ signs in Tiburon
item.description: Fifteen street signs bearing positive messages have gone up along Tiburon's waterfront and trails as part of a public-art project sponsored by the Heritage and Arts Commission.
item.content:encoded:
Scott Froschauer says the world is filled with “inherently alienating” advertising.

Whether people are being told to drink beer to have more fun or buy deodorant to smell better, he says, the underlying message is the same: “Because of these problems with you, you should get our product, and our product will solve these problems that you have.”

The Los Angeles-based artist says he’s trying to counteract that with a different kind of attention-grabbing medium: street signs.

Froschauer has installed 15 street signs bearing positive messages from downtown to Blackie’s Pasture and along the Old Rail Trail as part of his “The Word on the Street” exhibit. The signs started going up June 1 and will stay in place until early December. A dedication ceremony and meet-the-artist event is set for 12:30-2:30 p.m. June 6 at Shoreline Park near the Tiburon ferry landing.

“Rather than using it to tell you that you’re lacking something and you should buy a product, I’m going to use that attention to tell you that you’re actually fine, and you don’t need a product,” Froschauer says.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Los Angeles-based artist Scott Froschauer has brought his street-sign art exhibit ‘The Word on the Street’ to Tiburon for a six-month run. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark)

item.title: Pride on the Tiburon Peninsula
item.description: Tiburon held its inaugural Pride Picnic at Shoreline Park on May 31, organized by local couple Suki and Katya Skye.
item.content:encoded: Attendees stretch at Shoreline Park during Tiburon’s inaugural Pride Picnic on May 31. The free gathering, organized by Tiburon couple Suki and Katya Skye, featured a DJ, a vintage-clothing vendor and tarot readings, with an after-party at Cinelounge. The Skyes hope it becomes an annual event. Below, organizer Suki Skye (left) greets Sister Viva L’Amour des Hommes of the Sisters of Perpetual Indulgence of San Francisco. (Ted McDonnell / For The Ark)
item.dc:creator: The Ark

item.title: Book Passage marks 50 years under the vision of its Strawberry founders
item.description: Elaine Petrocelli opened her first bookstore 50 years ago without knowing what a business plan was. Book Passage now hosts roughly 1,000 author events a year.
item.content:encoded:
Elaine Petrocelli has always been a voracious reader.

Some 50 years ago, on a whim, she picked up and read a book on how to open a bookstore.

Petrocelli was looking to change careers and was inspired. The next morning, she told her husband, “This is it.”

“And then I went to my book club and they said, ‘Well, how are you going to do this? You have to write a business plan,’” Petrocelli recalls. “I said, ‘What’s a business plan?’”

Petrocelli wasn’t fazed. With her husband, Bill, she went on to open Book Passage, an independent bookstore that now has locations in San Francisco and Corte Madera and grew into one of the Bay Area’s most prominent literary landmarks.

On June 6, Petrocelli will reflect on her half-century in the bookselling industry in conversation with author and Stanford design-school educator Lisa Kay Solomon at the Corte Madera store. The conversation will also be streamed online.

That event kicks off wider 50th anniversary celebrations that will culminate with an event the weekend before Thanksgiving to mark the anniversary of when she first opened a bookstore in Larkspur that would later become Book Passage.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Strawberry residents Bill and Elaine Petrocelli, who own independent bookstore Book Passage, mark 50 years of being in the bookselling business this year. Pictured here at their Corte Madera store May 28, the Petrocellis have made Book Passage renowned for hosting or sponsoring book talks and lectures with speakers from a wide array of fields, from Belvedere author Isabel Allende to travel writer Rick Steves and U.S. Sen. Bernie Sanders to TV host Rachel Maddow. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark)

item.title: Debate grows in Belvedere over private improvements on the Strip
item.description: The Strip was deeded to Belvedere in 1897 for public waterfront use. A retaining wall collapse reignited debate over who is responsible for upkeep.
item.content:encoded:
A strip of low-tide beach along Belvedere Cove is at the center of a debate over how much the city should demand of homeowners who build private structures on public land — and what the public’s getting in return.

The shoreline in question is the Strip, a narrow corridor of city-owned land just south of the San Francisco Yacht Club parking lot. It runs a third of a mile from 172 Beach Road, near the Harry B. Allen public walking lane, to 340 Beach Road. Accessible on foot only at low tide, the three-parcel Strip was deeded to the city by Belvedere Land Co. in 1897 for use as a public waterfront, with language requiring that no structure impede “free passage of pedestrians from one end of The Strip to the other.”

The deed includes a reversion clause: if its conditions are violated, the land reverts to the land company.

Over the decades, homeowners along Beach Road have built stairs, retaining walls, fences, decks, hillevators and landings on the Strip, mostly to reach their private docks in the cove. In all, 17 of 21 residential parcels bordering the Strip hold revocable licenses, the city’s standard tool for authorizing private improvements on public land. The licenses can be revoked at any time at the council’s discretion.

At the City Council’s May 11 meeting, Planning and Building Director Rebecca Markwick told the council the public benefit is hard to pin down.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: Belvedere’s public shoreline includes a series of three parcels along Belvedere Cove collectively known as the Strip — roughly outlined in yellow — which spans from 172 Beach Road, near the Harry B. Allen public walking lane, to 340 Beach Road. Belvedere Land Co. deeded the Strip to the city in 1897 with restrictions prohibiting private structures other than wharves, boathouses or bathhouses. (Kevin Hessel graphic / The Ark; Apple Maps)

item.title: Belvedere-Tiburon Library budget boosts programs while projecting modest surplus
item.description: The Belvedere-Tiburon Library is proposing a $3.78M budget for fiscal 2027, projecting a $16,000 surplus and a nearly 4% cost-of-living raise for staff.
item.content:encoded:
The Belvedere-Tiburon Library plans to boost spending on programming for adults, children and teens next fiscal year while giving staff a nearly 4% cost-of-living raise under a $3.78 million draft budget that all but breaks even.

The library agency board of trustees reviewed the draft budget at its May 18 meeting. The board will vote to formally adopt the fiscal 2026-2027 spending plan at its June 15 meeting, and the new fiscal year begins July 1.

The budget projects $3.92 million in revenue, $122,300 of which will go toward bond-debt service, leaving $3.8 million in operating revenue against $3.78 million in expenditures, a $16,001 surplus. Library Director Crystal Duran called it an “almost status quo” year.

Cash balances by the end of the current 2025-2026 fiscal year are projected at $2.68 million, and library agency policy requires 20% of annual operating expenditures to be set aside for an operating reserve. Fiscal 2027’s target for that reserve is about $757,000.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Everyday Encounters: A youth ambassador walks to support clean-water initiatives
item.description: Leila Al-Moosa and classmates carried water jugs four miles to highlight the burden on girls in developing countries.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon teen Leila Al‑Moosa was in fifth grade when she organized her firstfundraiser for WaterAid, an international nongovernmental organization that provides clean drinking water and hygiene education to countries such as Ethiopia, India and Zambia.

She and 19 of her peers at Marin Country Day School completed the organization’s “Walk for Water Challenge,” carrying 2.5-gallon containers of water for four miles, representing the average distance that women and girls in developing countries walk daily to collect water. Their goal was to raise $10,000.

“Women will have to leave school in about third grade and completely stop their education in impoverished places and families in order to go walk and collect water,” she says.

Now 15 and a freshman at The Branson School in Ross, Leila is a youth ambassador for WaterAid. She continues to participate annually in the “Walk for Water Challenge,” completing this year’s challenge May 31.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: Tiburon resident Leila Al-Moosa, 15, walks her dog May 27 at Blackie’s Pasture. A freshman at The Branson School in Ross, Al-Moosa is a youth ambassador for WaterAid. (Tyler Callister / The Ark)
